channel.title: Blogs on Olatunji Profile
channel.description: Recent content in Blogs on Olatunji Profile

item.title: Is an Anonymous Survey Really Anonymous? A Data Ethics Perspective
item.description: A Data Ethics Perspective Have you ever been asked to complete an “anonymous” survey, only to realize you first have to log in with your username and password? At first, that might not seem like a big deal. After all, organizations often require users to log in to verify their identity. But if you’re told your responses are anonymous, would you truly believe it? More importantly, would you feel comfortable giving honest feedback? This situation raises some interesting ethical questions, especially from a data science perspective. In this scenario, participants complete a 10-question survey after finishing a course. They must log in using their organizational username and password before accessing the survey. Once inside, they are told that their responses will remain anonymous. Executive management plans to use the survey results to decide which courses should be redesigned or updated, and as the data analyst, I am responsible for analyzing the results and providing recommendations. At first glance, this seems like a reasonable process. However, when we look at it through the lens of data ethics, several concerns become apparent. Ethical data collection is not just about gathering information—it is about respecting participants, protecting their privacy, and ensuring the data collected is trustworthy enough to support good decisions.

item.title: When Smart Isn’t Fair: Rethinking Bias and Fairness in Artificial Intelligence
item.description:
Artificial intelligence (AI) has quickly become embedded in the systems that shape modern life—from entertainment platforms and healthcare diagnostics to hiring tools and financial decision making.

Because AI systems process vast amounts of data with speed and consistency, they are often perceived as objective. Yet this perception is misleading. AI systems can unintentionally reproduce or even intensify existing social inequalities, especially when they learn from biased human data. Understanding how algorithmic bias emerges—and how fairness can be meaningfully integrated into AI development—is essential for anyone working with or studying intelligent systems. This article examines the roots of algorithmic bias, the ethical principles that guide responsible AI, and the governance and data practices needed to build systems worthy of public trust.
